message:
> "Toxic masculinity"

Is a very real thing that nearly led me to suicide as a young teenager because it demanded I not show and express and talk about and analyze my emotions.

> It's hard to distinguish feminist rhetoric from the rhetoric of ethnic cleansers.

Sure, if you're viewing feminist rhetoric through the lens of anti-feminist echo chambers.

> That goes both ways. How do you know it's not women who are the privileged class? There is a documented bias in both men and women to favor women. That bias could be and probably is informing you more than you realize.

Which group makes up the vast majority of top earners and legislators?

> Tell that to a man who isn't doing fine. Or are you saying that doesn't exist?

Having privilege doesn't mean you won't have a rough time; it means it'll be easier for you to transition from a rough time to a better time.

The point is, why is it called "toxic masculinity" when it's meaning refers to neither something masculine, nor even necessarily something toxic while its female counterpart is called "internalized misogyny"? 

Shouldn't it be called "internalized misandry" or perhaps even "female privilege"?
personality_characteristics: ['Active', 'Prefers to spend his weekends camping and hiking', 'Republican']

message: @interior-survey I get where you're coming from, but calling it "toxic masculinity" isn't just about men being bad—it's about how society pushes those expectations on everyone who's male. And yeah, it's easy to flip the script and say women are the ones with privilege, but the data on leadership and paychecks still shows a huge gap. Maybe we're all a little biased, but pretending it's a level playing field for everyone isn't realistic yet.

@shabby-pole I get where you're coming from, but calling it "toxic masculinity" actually feels like a way to shut down the conversation by making men the only culprits. It's like saying all problems in the world are men's fault. Maybe we should talk about how both sides can push harmful ideas, instead of just blaming one group.

@parallel-anthology Wow, so now we're blaming both sides equally? That's a fresh take. I guess if you split the toxic stuff 50/50, nobody gets in trouble. Maybe we should just call it "toxic behavior" and stop making it about gender. Or am I missing the point again? 😂

@legacy-truss I don't think splitting the blame makes things better. If we ignore how gender shapes expectations and behaviors, we're just papering over the cracks. It's not about being "missing the point"—it's about noticing which patterns actually hurt people.

@legacy-truss I get where you're coming from, but saying we just call it "toxic behavior" without pointing fingers misses the real issues. Like, sure, people of any gender can be jerks, but when the system is set up to favor one over the other, that's not equal. It's like saying a broken window is just bad glass without looking at the structure holding it up.

@rounded-envelope I don't buy the idea that blaming the system is the same as blaming individuals. If we keep shifting the blame around, nothing gets fixed. We need to actually address the structures that make toxicity possible in the first place.

message:
>For evil is the absence of goodness, and all goodness comes from love (of God) either directly or indirectly.

It might be convenient for your position to define things this way, but there's no good reason to believe it's true.

Well that's the Christian position on the reality of good and evil. It's nearly an axiom. God is all goodness, all life, and all joy. Evil is a lack of goodness, of life, and true joy. Just as blindness is not really a thing but a lack of something - sight. Darkness is a lack of light. Cold is a lack of heat. 

That is my claim. What do you think about good and evil?

message:
Eh- that's not *entirely* true.

Is there honestly any difference between dressing up as a Samurai vs a Mohawk warrior? Why is one considered "ok" while the other is shamed for being racist?

Sure, some aspects of a costume may be considered sacred to whatever nation you're portraying, but realistically if we're going to go down that path we have to do it for *everyone*. You don't get to decide that a white guy dressing up as a Buddhist monk is OK while a guy dressing up as an aboriginal shaman is racist.

Either *nobody* can portray *any* cultural or ethnic group or everyone can portray whoever they like.

>There is a difference between dressing up as a Geisha or a ninja and dressing up as an Indian.

Dressing up as an "indian" means wearing clothes historically connected to aboriginal peoples. Unless they're putting on brownface, what they're doing is absolutely *no* different than someone wearing a toga.

>It's science because there are departments that study social science in most Universities, and it is considered to be science within the wider academic community.

Same thing can be said for Christian science. Christian science is also considered to be science within the academic community of Christian scientists.

People don't believe in science because it is science and others believe in it. That is how religions and cults work, not science (and yes, "scientism" is a cult that is prevalent in today's world... which is what Christian scientists try to cash on).

Science produces repeatable experiments with reproducible results. Nobody has to agree with it. People agreeing or disagreeing with it does not change it. How people feel about it does not change it. If it does not rely on experiments or well founded theories (i.e. the theories which have not been falsified despite many experiments trying to do so) and if it is not repeatable and reproducible, it is not science.

Seriously?  Social science isn't science because it is *called* science.  It's science because there are departments that study social science in most Universities, and it is considered to be science within the wider academic community.

I guess the difference in my eyes is that since it is meaningless, it’s just easier to be referred to as my gender I was raised as. I would like to live in a word where it is so unnecessary to adhere to societal pressure on gender roles, but I know we don’t live in that world. I think that the people in the NBC link and others like them want to reach that same world as me, but they see a different approach. I think it may be more beneficial to raise children as their “assigned” gender but encourage them to seek activities that interest them regardless of societal stereotypes.

> I would like to live in a word where it is so unnecessary to adhere to societal pressure on gender roles, but I know we don’t live in that world.

The only reason we don't live in that world is because it this current world is perpetually being reinforced. Gender-fluid people believe so strongly in the benefits of a world without gender (ie. major drop in sexism), that they are willing to suffer the societal consequences (people judging them and name-calling) in order bring this concept to the forefront of culture.

@oriented-calico I get where you're coming from, but honestly, it feels like people keep trying to rewrite the rules while everyone else is just stuck playing by the old ones. I don't think most folks are ready to ditch gender roles overnight — it's too tied up in how we raise our kids and what society expects. Maybe instead of fighting for total change, we should at least let kids pick their hobbies and interests freely, regardless of what a brochure says.

@ferocious-flannel Oh great, now you're saying we should just let kids do whatever they want, but then who's going to tell them not to glue their hands to the ceiling or start a boy band at 40? Maybe we need a little structure, or else we'll end up with a generation of people who can't even decide if they're a pizza or a cloud.

This would only provoke the rapid advance of automation. The minute the California minimum wage passed to $15 an hour my local walmart fired 32 cashiers and brought in machines that don't take sick days, ask for raises or demand benefits, and now there are 7 cashiers. 3 for the 21+ line 2 to manage theft in self checkout and 2 for Garden checkout.

As it stands right now most companies already pay above the minimum wage. It's not much, + or - 25 cents, but they pay what the market requires of the job. I really hate to tell you, but being a cashier at walmart is necessarily worth less than it was 30 years ago. It's certainly not worth more, when a robot can do a person's job for next to nothing, and once the robot is paid off does not require much additional investment unlike living people.

Forcing a national minimum wage will just spook wealth overseas, where the regulations are not as cost prohibitive and the money is just as good. Then every American citizen loses. Being able to pay some of your bills is better than an income of $0 because you can't find work.

>"subjective" is a better word than "debatable" IMO because it's more a matter of opinion regarding interpretation, than a matter of opinion regarding fact.

Interpretations are accountable to facts. Is it a fact that misogyny is a word with a reasonably clear and easily accessible definition? Is it also a fact that most (if not all) of the MRA subs have a high number of regular contributors who frequently promote attitudes and claims that very clearly fit the bill? These may not be easy questions to answer, but it doesn't follow that definitive answers don't exist even in principle.

Opinions aren't automatically respectable just because they are opinions. They can be weak, unjustified, misguided, or totally out of key with reality. They can even be flat out wrong. When I suggest that a claim isn't "debatable," I mean that there is no sensible, persuasive argument to be made against it based on the facts commonly available to us. So no, "subjective" is not a better word in this case.

You, in your post and other comments, define MRA subs as espousing outright hatred of women. And you said in another comment that you disagree with them -- even the ones you don't consider bastards -- on almost everything. So he's actually got quite a reason to suspect that he's not gonna change your view because you have very broad and rigid definitions of misogyny and outright hatred etc..

More importantly; you didn't even address the part of his comment where he explains how Reddit *is* doing something about it. Reddit is not allowing these subs to do as they wish; they follow and enforce strict rules such as not allowing doxxing and threatening that subreddit if their rhetoric goes to far.

The argument put forward seems like "No True Scotsman" - the people you are condemning claim to be Christian, attend Christian churches, follow Christian pastors (who espouse the same views they hold), etc.  They _are_ Christians, though perhaps not the specific sect of Christian that you are.

Now, that aside, the problem with scripture as I see it is that we can cherry pick quite a bit to justify your possitions.  Sure, Jesus told us to take care of the sick, the widows and the children, but he also said "He who will not work shall not eat" in Thessalonians 3:10.  That can be used to justify a stance against welfare despite the message of love elsewhere in the Bible; Jesus himself said you shouldn't expect to get help from others if you are not willing to work yourself.  Similarly, the letters of Paul (which are canonical in Christianity) have a great deal to say about many of of the things that you are calling out as non compassionate - while you can love the person and their soul, Christians are _commanded_ to try and stop behavior that is immoral.

In summation, they may not fit your personal definition of Christian, but that doesn't make them unChristian.

I don't believe it is a logical fallacy, because my belief is that Republican values are not Christian values.  

Going against a Christian value is unchristian, regardless of how you label the individual. 

To avoid the problem of responses that cherry pick scripture, I defined Christianity in terms of its greatest, or core, principle, as defined by Jesus.  Those who are angry or hateful toward homosexuals are objectively unchristian in that behavior.  If you disagree on this point, I don't think your arguments will be persuasive.  

Lastly, Christians are not commanded to create laws to punish anyone who disagrees with them.  I don't believe those who approach the problem this way are really concerned with helping the individual.  We don't lock people up for years because we want to help them.

@shabby-pole I get where you're coming from, but calling anyone who attends church a non-Christian just because they don't match your narrow view is a bit hypocritical. If the Bible says love your neighbor, then judging people for their faith journey seems off. Maybe we should focus on helping instead of gatekeeping who's "real."
